modules.name: Cascade
modules.description: Why do some protests ignite and others fizzle? Granovetter's 1978 threshold model: each agent has a personal threshold of how many *others* must already be acting before they join in. A 'rabid' population with one threshold-0 zealot will cascade from almost no external pressure; an otherwise identical population without that one zealot will not. The ignition point is set not by average sentiment but by the lowest threshold in the chain. Outputs per-agent activation gates, active fraction, delta-active-per-tick, and an ignition pulse.

modules.name: Discourse
modules.description: How do echo chambers form? Deffuant–Weisbuch bounded-confidence dynamics: agents only update their opinion when they meet someone already close to them. Set the confidence threshold ε high (broad open-mindedness) and the population converges to a single consensus; set ε low (narrow tolerance) and it fragments into several stable opinion clusters that no longer talk to each other. A live model of polarization, opinion clustering, and the formation of incompatible 'epistemic bubbles'. Outputs polyphonic per-agent values, mean, variance, cluster count.

modules.name: Pareto
modules.description: Where does wealth inequality come from? Even with *fair* coin flips and no inherent advantages, pairwise random trading produces a winner-take-all distribution — the 'yard-sale model' of Chakraborti (2002) and Boghosian (2014). No bias, no skill, no fraud: just trading. Start with everyone equal and watch the Gini coefficient climb from 0 toward 1 as wealth condenses on a single agent. Adding BIAS (advantage to the larger pile) accelerates concentration; adding POOL (mean-field redistribution) creates non-trivial steady states. Outputs polyphonic value shares, Gini, top share, concentration gate.

modules.name: Dilemma
modules.description: Can cooperation evolve from purely selfish agents? Axelrod's 1980 round-robin Prisoner's Dilemma tournament showed: yes, if relationships repeat. Dilemma runs an iterated PD between four classic strategies (ALL_C, ALL_D, TFT, GRIM). Tit-for-tat (TFT) wins in a quiet world by being nice, provokable, and forgiving. Crank the NOISE knob and watch cooperation collapse: a single misread action between two TFTs starts a defection feud that neither can stop. A live demonstration that cooperation is real but fragile.

modules.name: Diffusion
modules.description: Why does every viral product look like an S-curve? Bass's 1969 diffusion-of-innovations model: a small minority adopts spontaneously (innovation rate p), the rest adopt by imitating already-adopters (imitation rate q × adopter fraction). The result is the canonical S — slow start driven by innovators, accelerating middle driven by imitators, plateau as the pool of non-adopters thins. Patch a Polis/Network module to Diffusion's left and the dynamics switch from well-mixed mass-action to graph-aware peer transmission: hubs adopt early on a Barabási–Albert network and dramatically accelerate the curve. Outputs per-agent adoption gates, fraction adopted, adoption rate, saturation gate.

modules.name: Network
modules.description: Relational structure generator. Builds an N-agent undirected graph in one of three canonical models: Watts-Strogatz small-world (ring lattice with β rewiring), Erdős–Rényi random graph (each edge present with probability β), or Barabási–Albert preferential attachment (scale-free growth, K seed connections per new node). Outputs polyphonic per-agent degree, mean degree, global clustering coefficient, and a connected-graph gate. The visualization shows the network laid out on a circle with edges between nodes — useful both standalone and as a structural pre-requisite for diffusion-on-networks pedagogy.
